From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Amateur observation of birds

For other uses, see [Birdwatching (disambiguation)](/wiki/Birdwatching_(disambiguation) "Birdwatching (disambiguation)").

"Birding" and "Birdwatchers" redirect here. For the hunting of birds, see [Fowling](/wiki/Fowling "Fowling"). For the capture of birds, see [Bird trapping](/wiki/Bird_trapping "Bird trapping"). For the magazine, see [Birding (magazine)](/wiki/Birding_(magazine) "Birding (magazine)"). For the film, see [Birdwatchers (film)](/wiki/Birdwatchers_(film) "Birdwatchers (film)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/NMAAHC_Bird_Walk.jpg/250px-NMAAHC_Bird_Walk.jpg)](/wiki/File:NMAAHC_Bird_Walk.jpg)

Three people birdwatching with binoculars

**Birdwatching**, or **birding**, is the [observing](/wiki/Wildlife_observation "Wildlife observation") of [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird"), either as a recreational activity or as a form of [citizen science](/wiki/Citizen_science "Citizen science"). A birdwatcher may observe by using their naked eye, by using a visual enhancement device such as [binoculars](/wiki/Binoculars "Binoculars") or a [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope"), by listening for bird sounds,[[1]](#cite_note-dunne-1)[[2]](#cite_note-oddie-2) watching public [webcams](/wiki/Webcam "Webcam"), or by viewing smart bird feeder cameras.

Most birdwatchers pursue this activity for recreational or social reasons, unlike [ornithologists](/wiki/Ornithology "Ornithology"), who engage in the study of birds using formal scientific methods.[[1]](#cite_note-dunne-1)[[2]](#cite_note-oddie-2)

## Birding, birdwatching, and twitching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Birding, birdwatching, and twitching")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Keskisenlampi_birdwatching_tower.jpg/250px-Keskisenlampi_birdwatching_tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Keskisenlampi_birdwatching_tower.jpg)

A birdwatching tower in [Hankasalmi](/wiki/Hankasalmi "Hankasalmi"), Finland

The first recorded use of the term *birdwatcher* was in 1712 by William Oldsworth. The term *birding* was also used for the practice of *fowling* or hunting with firearms as in [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s *[The Merry Wives of Windsor](/wiki/The_Merry_Wives_of_Windsor "The Merry Wives of Windsor")* (1602): "She laments sir... her husband goes this morning a-birding."[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss200433-3) The terms *birding* and *birdwatching* are today used by some interchangeably, although some participants prefer *birding*, partly because it includes the [auditory](/wiki/Hearing_(sense) "Hearing (sense)") aspects of enjoying birds.

In North America, many birders differentiate themselves from birdwatchers, and the term *birder* is unfamiliar to most lay people. At the most basic level, the distinction is perceived as one of dedication or intensity, though this is a subjective differentiation. Generally, self-described birders perceive themselves to be more versed in minutiae such as identification (aural and visual), molt, distribution, migration timing, and habitat usage. Whereas these dedicated *birders* may often travel specifically in search of birds, *birdwatchers* have been described by some enthusiasts as having a more limited scope, perhaps not venturing far from their own yards or local parks to view birds.[[1]](#cite_note-dunne-1) Indeed, in 1969 a *Birding Glossary* appeared in *[Birding](/wiki/Birding_(magazine) "Birding (magazine)")* magazine which gave the following definitions:

> **Birder**. The acceptable term used to describe the person who seriously pursues the hobby of birding. May be professional or amateur.
>
> **Birding**. A hobby in which individuals enjoy the challenge of bird study, listing, or other general activities involving bird life.
>
> **Bird-watcher**. A rather ambiguous term used to describe the person who watches birds for any reason at all, and should not be used to refer to the serious birder.

— *Birding*, Volume 1, No.2

*Twitching* is a British term used to mean "the pursuit of a previously located rare bird." In North America, it is more often called *chasing*. The term *twitcher*, sometimes misapplied as a synonym for *birder*, is reserved for those who travel long distances to see a rare bird that would then be *ticked*, or counted on a list.[[2]](#cite_note-oddie-2)[[4]](#cite_note-Dooley,_Sean_2007-4) The term originated in the 1950s, when it was used to describe the nervous behaviour of Howard Medhurst, a British birdwatcher.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) Earlier terms for those who chased rarities were *pot-hunter*, *tally-hunter*, or *tick-hunter*. The main goal of twitching is often to accumulate species on one's lists. Some birders engage in competition to accumulate the longest species list. The act of the pursuit itself is referred to as a *twitch* or a *chase*. A rare bird that stays long enough for people to see it is *twitchable* or *chaseable*.[[2]](#cite_note-oddie-2)[[4]](#cite_note-Dooley,_Sean_2007-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)

Twitching is highly developed in the United Kingdom, the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"). The size of these countries makes it possible to travel throughout them quickly and with relative ease. The most popular twitches in the UK have drawn large crowds; for example, approximately 2,500 people travelled to [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), to view a [golden-winged warbler](/wiki/Golden-winged_warbler "Golden-winged warbler") (*Vermivora chrysoptera*), which is native to North America.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Twitchers have developed [their own vocabulary](/wiki/Twitchers%27_vocabulary "Twitchers' vocabulary"). For example, a twitcher who fails to see a rare bird has *dipped out*; if other twitchers do see the bird, they may feel *gripped off*. *Suppression* is the act of concealing news of a rare bird from other twitchers.[[2]](#cite_note-oddie-2)

Many birders maintain a *[life list](/wiki/Life_list "Life list")*, that is, a list of all of the species they have seen in their life, usually with details about the sighting such as date and location. The [American Birding Association](/wiki/American_Birding_Association "American Birding Association") has specific rules about how a bird species may be documented and recorded in such a list if it is submitted to the ABA; however, the criteria for the personal recording of these lists are very subjective. Some birders "count" species they have identified audibly, while others only record species that they have identified visually. Some also maintain a *country list*, *state list*, *county list*, *yard list*, *year list*, or any combination of these.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Birdwatchers%2C_AH_Chisholm%2C_State_Library_NSW.jpg/250px-Birdwatchers%2C_AH_Chisholm%2C_State_Library_NSW.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birdwatchers,_AH_Chisholm,_State_Library_NSW.jpg)

Bird photography, New South Wales, June 1921 (A. H. Chisholm)

The early interest in observing birds for their aesthetic rather than utilitarian (mainly food) value is traced to the late 18th century in the works of [Gilbert White](/wiki/Gilbert_White "Gilbert White"), [Thomas Bewick](/wiki/Thomas_Bewick "Thomas Bewick"), [George Montagu](/wiki/George_Montagu_(naturalist) "George Montagu (naturalist)") and [John Clare](/wiki/John_Clare "John Clare").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss200410-9) The study of birds, and of natural history in general, became increasingly prevalent in Britain during the [Victorian Era](/wiki/Victorian_Era "Victorian Era"), often associated with [collection](/wiki/Bird_collections "Bird collections"), eggs and later skins being the artifacts of interest. Wealthy collectors made use of their contacts in the [colonies](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") to obtain specimens from around the world. It was only in the late 19th century that the call for bird protection led to the rising popularity of observations of living birds. The [Audubon Society](/wiki/Audubon_Society "Audubon Society") was started to protect birds from the growing trade in feathers in the United States while the [Royal Society for the Protection of Birds](/wiki/Royal_Society_for_the_Protection_of_Birds "Royal Society for the Protection of Birds") began in Britain.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss200472-10)

The phrase "bird watching" appeared for the first time as the title of the book *Bird Watching* by [Edmund Selous](/wiki/Edmund_Selous "Edmund Selous") in 1901.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss200488-11) In North America, the identification of birds, once thought possible only by shooting, was made possible by the emergence of optics and field identification guides. The earliest field guide in the US was *Birds through an Opera Glass* (1889) by [Florence Bailey](/wiki/Florence_Augusta_Merriam_Bailey "Florence Augusta Merriam Bailey").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Birding in North America was focused in the early and mid-20th century in the eastern seaboard region, and was influenced by the works of [Ludlow Griscom](/wiki/Ludlow_Griscom "Ludlow Griscom") and later [Roger Tory Peterson](/wiki/Roger_Tory_Peterson "Roger Tory Peterson"). *[Bird Neighbors](/wiki/Bird_Neighbors "Bird Neighbors")* (1897) by [Neltje Blanchan](/wiki/Neltje_Blanchan "Neltje Blanchan"), an early birding book, sold over 250,000 copies.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was illustrated with color photographs of stuffed birds.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The organization and networking of those interested in birds began through organizations like the Audubon Society, which was against the killing of birds, and the [American Ornithologists' Union](/wiki/American_Ornithologists%27_Union "American Ornithologists' Union") (AOU). The availability of first the bicycle and then the car increased the mobility of birdwatchers and this made new locations accessible.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004104–106-15) Networks of birdwatchers in the UK began to form in the late 1930s under the [British Trust for Ornithology](/wiki/British_Trust_for_Ornithology "British Trust for Ornithology") (BTO). The BTO saw the potential to produce scientific results through the networks, unlike the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds (RSPB) which like the Audubon Society originated from the bird protection movement.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Like the AOU in North America, the BOU had a focus mainly on collection-based taxonomy. The BOU changed focus to ecology and behaviour only in the 1940s.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The BTO movement towards 'organized birdwatching' was opposed by the RSPB, which claimed that the 'scientification' of the pastime was 'undesirable'. This stand was to change only in 1936 when the RSPB was taken over by [Tom Harrisson](/wiki/Tom_Harrisson "Tom Harrisson") and others. Harrisson was instrumental in the organization of pioneering surveys of the [great crested grebe](/wiki/Great_crested_grebe "Great crested grebe").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004128-18)

Increased mobility of birdwatchers ensured that books like *Where to Watch Birds* by [John Gooders](/wiki/John_Gooders "John Gooders") became best-sellers.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004233–234-19) By the 1960s air travel became feasible and long-distance holiday destinations opened up. By 1965, Britain's first birding tour company, *Ornitholidays* had been started by Lawrence Holloway.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004234–235-20) Travelling far away also led to problems in name usage: British birds such as "wheatear", "heron" and "swallow" needed adjectives to differentiate them in places where there were several related species.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004250-21) The falling cost of air travel made flying to remote birding destinations a possibility for a large number of people towards the 1980s. The need for global guides to birds increased, and one of the biggest resulting projects was the *[Handbook of the Birds of the World](/wiki/Handbook_of_the_Birds_of_the_World "Handbook of the Birds of the World")*, begun in the 1990s by Josep del Hoyo, Jordi Sargatal, David A. Christie, and ornithologist Andy Elliott.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004252–253-22)

Initially, birdwatching was largely restricted to developed countries such as the United Kingdom and the United States of America. Since the second half of the 20th century an increasing number of people in developing countries have engaged in this activity, such as in the [Degua Tembien](/wiki/Degua_Tembien "Degua Tembien") district of Ethiopia.[[23]](#cite_note-ALN2019-23) Transnational birding has played an important role in this, as birders in developing countries usually take up the pastime under the influence of foreign cultures with a history of birding.[[24]](#cite_note-24) A majority of transnational birders are middle-aged, male, affluent, and belong to the Anglophone countries or Scandinavia.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Economic and environmental impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Economic and environmental impact")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Antioquia_Brushfinch_imported_from_iNaturalist_photo_53700275_on_1_October_2021.jpg/250px-Antioquia_Brushfinch_imported_from_iNaturalist_photo_53700275_on_1_October_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antioquia_Brushfinch_imported_from_iNaturalist_photo_53700275_on_1_October_2021.jpg)

Spotting rare birds, such as the [Antioquia brushfinch](/wiki/Antioquia_brushfinch "Antioquia brushfinch"), is a goal for some birdwatchers.

In the 20th century, most of the birding activity in North America was done on the east coast.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The publication of Roger Tory Peterson's field guide in 1934 led to the initial increase in birding. Binoculars, an essential piece of birding equipment, became more easily available after World War II, making the hobby more accessible. The practice of travelling long distances to see rare bird species was aided by the availability of cars and their associated infrastructure.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004265-27) Nevertheless, distance to urban centres may still affect number of birdwatchers participating in observations of rare bird species.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) About 4% of North Americans were interested in birding in the 1970s, and in the mid-1980s at least 11% were found to watch birds at least 20 days of the year. The number of birders was estimated at 61 million in the late 1980s. The income level of birders has been found to be well above average.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

*The Sibley Guide to Birds*, published in 2000, had sold 500,000 copies by 2002.[[29]](#cite_note-29) It was found that the number of birdwatchers rose, but there appeared to be a drop in birdwatching in the backyard.[[30]](#cite_note-usfws-30)

According to a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service study, birders contributed $36 billion to the US economy in 2006, and one fifth (20%) of all Americans are identified as birdwatchers.[[31]](#cite_note-sof-31) According to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in 2016, over 45 million Americans consider themselves birders.[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32)

North American birders were estimated to have spent as much as US$32 billion in 2001.[[30]](#cite_note-usfws-30) The spending is on the rise around the world. Kuşcenneti National Park (KNP) at Lake Manyas, a [Ramsar site](/wiki/Ramsar_site "Ramsar site") in Turkey, was estimated to attract birders who spent as much as US$103,320,074 annually.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Guided bird tours have become a major business, with at least 127 companies offering tours worldwide. An average trip to a less-developed country costs $4,000 per person and includes about 12 participants for each of 150 trips a year. It has been suggested that this economic potential needs to be tapped for conservation.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Birdwatching tourism is considered to be one of the fastest-growing nature-based tourism sectors in the world, often involving well-educated or wealthy travelers with specific interests in the places they visit.[[35]](#cite_note-Small_sight—Big_might:_Economic_imp-35) In addition to this, birdwatching tourism is considered a niche market of nature-based tourism. Birdwatching and other niche tourism markets are good for market diversification and mitigating the impacts of seasonality in a tourism market as well as bringing economic resources to remote communities, thus diversifying their economies and contributing to biodiversity conservation.[[35]](#cite_note-Small_sight—Big_might:_Economic_imp-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) It is estimated that birdwatching ecotourism contributes $41 billion per year to the U.S. economy.[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32) The large funds generated by birdwatching ecotourism have been suggested as a replacement for tax revenue generated by bird hunting which has dropped to its lowest levels in decades.[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32)

Birding ecotourism companies are also making contributions to conservation. Birding Ecotours, which runs both international and domestic trips, donates a minimum of 10% of its net profits to bird conservation and communities it operates in.[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32) Another tour operator, Hardy Boat, has donated $200,000 to Project Puffin to conserve puffin populations off the Atlantic Coast.[[32]](#cite_note-:0-32)

One of the expectations of [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") is that the travels of birders to a place will contribute to the improvement of the local economy, ensuring that the environment is valued and protected. Birdwatchers contribute to conservation, helping build and disseminate environmental knowledge by participating in citizen science. However, birding can bring about an increased penetration of [ecosystem services](/wiki/Ecosystem_service "Ecosystem service") that are perceived as birdwatchers' indispensable attributes. By their presence and obstinacy, birdwatchers affect the attractiveness of the breeding migration or roosting sites for birds, flush birds, and otherwise increase the pressure on birds and their habitats (e.g., luring birds out of their hideouts and stressing them by playing their calls or exposing birds and their nests to predators).[[37]](#cite_note-37) Furthermore, other impacts include disturbance to birds, the environment, local cultures[[23]](#cite_note-ALN2019-23) and the economy. Methods to reduce negative impact and improve the value of conservation are the subject of research.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Activities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Conowingo_dam_shooters_fishing_pier_%2814106368940%29.jpg/250px-Conowingo_dam_shooters_fishing_pier_%2814106368940%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conowingo_dam_shooters_fishing_pier_(14106368940).jpg)

Photographers at [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland")'s [Conowingo Dam](/wiki/Conowingo_Dam "Conowingo Dam"), a popular winter location for seeing [bald eagles](/wiki/Bald_eagles "Bald eagles").

Many birders occupy themselves with observing local species (birding in their "local patch"[[39]](#cite_note-39)), but may also make specific trips to observe birds in other locales. The most active times of the year for birding in [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate") zones are during the spring or fall [migrations](/wiki/Bird_migration "Bird migration") when the greatest variety of birds may be seen. On these occasions, large numbers of birds travel north or south to wintering or nesting locations. Early mornings are typically better as the birds are more active and vocal making them easier to spot.

Certain locations such as a local patch of forest, wetland and coast may be favoured according to the location and season. [Seawatching](/wiki/Seawatching "Seawatching"), or pelagic birding, is a type of birding where observers based at a coastal watch point, such as a headland, watch birds flying over the sea. This is one form of [pelagic](/wiki/Pelagic "Pelagic") birding, though birders also seek pelagic species from seagoing vessels.

Weather plays an important role in the occurrence of rare birds. In Britain, suitable wind conditions may lead to [drift migration](/wiki/Drift_migration "Drift migration"), and an influx of birds from the east. In North America, birds caught in the tail-end of a hurricane may be blown inland.[[40]](#cite_note-moss-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Stretto_di_messina_-_bottleneck%2C_sicilia%2C_from_the_peloritani.jpg/250px-Stretto_di_messina_-_bottleneck%2C_sicilia%2C_from_the_peloritani.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stretto_di_messina_-_bottleneck,_sicilia,_from_the_peloritani.jpg)

The [Strait of Messina](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina "Strait of Messina"), [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), a classic [migration](/wiki/Bird_migration "Bird migration") bottleneck, seen from the [Peloritani](/wiki/Peloritani "Peloritani") mountains

### Monitoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Monitoring")]

Birders may take part in censuses of bird populations and migratory patterns which are sometimes specific to individual species. These birdwatchers may also count all birds in a given area, as in the [Christmas Bird Count](/wiki/Christmas_Bird_Count "Christmas Bird Count"), or follow carefully designed study protocols. This kind of [citizen science](/wiki/Citizen_science "Citizen science") can assist in identifying environmental threats to the well-being of birds or, conversely, in assessing outcomes of environmental management initiatives intended to ensure the survival of at-risk species or to encourage the breeding of species for aesthetic or ecological reasons.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

This more scientific side of the hobby is an aspect of ornithology, coordinated in the UK by the [British Trust for Ornithology](/wiki/British_Trust_for_Ornithology "British Trust for Ornithology"). The [Cornell Lab of Ornithology](/wiki/Cornell_Lab_of_Ornithology "Cornell Lab of Ornithology") hosts many citizen-science projects to track the number and distribution of bird species across North America. These surveys help scientists note major changes from year to year which may occur as a result of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), disease, predation, and other factors.[[42]](#cite_note-texas-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Environmental education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Environmental education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Observaci%C3%B3n_de_aves_en_Nador.JPG/250px-Observaci%C3%B3n_de_aves_en_Nador.JPG)](/wiki/File:Observaci%C3%B3n_de_aves_en_Nador.JPG)

Moroccan students watching birds at Nador's lagoon as a part of [environmental education](/wiki/Environmental_education "Environmental education") activities organized by the [Spanish Ornithological Society](/wiki/Spanish_Ornithological_Society "Spanish Ornithological Society")

Because of their accessibility and ubiquity, birds are a useful tool for [environmental education](/wiki/Environmental_education "Environmental education") and awareness of environmental issues. Birdwatching can increase respect for nature and awareness of the fragility of [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem").

### Competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Competition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Birders_at_Caerlaverock.jpg/250px-Birders_at_Caerlaverock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birders_at_Caerlaverock.jpg)

Birdwatchers watching Britain's fifth-ever [white-tailed lapwing](/wiki/White-tailed_lapwing "White-tailed lapwing") at [Caerlaverock](/wiki/WWT_Caerlaverock "WWT Caerlaverock"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), 6 June 2007

Birding as a competitive event is organized in some parts of the world.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Such competitions encourage individuals or teams to accumulate large numbers of species within a specified time or area with special rules. Some birdwatchers will also compete by attempting to increase their life list, national list, state list, provincial list, county list, or [year list](/wiki/Big_year "Big year"). The [American Birding Association](/wiki/American_Birding_Association "American Birding Association") was originally started as a club for "listers", but it now serves a much broader audience. Still, the ABA continues to publish an official annual report of North American list standings.

Competitive birdwatching events include:

* Big Day: teams have 24 hours to identify as many species as possible.
* [Big Year](/wiki/Big_year "Big year"): like a big day, but contestants are individuals, and need to be prepared to invest a great deal of time and money.
* Big Sit or Big Stay: birdwatchers must see birds from a circle of prescribed diameter (e.g.: 17 feet[[45]](#cite_note-45)). Once birds are spotted, birdwatchers can leave the circle to confirm the identity, but new birds seen may not be counted.
* [Christmas Bird Count](/wiki/Christmas_Bird_Count "Christmas Bird Count"): See as many birds as possible between December 14 and January 5.
* [World Series of Birding](/wiki/World_Series_of_Birding "World Series of Birding"): An annual birding competition organised by the New Jersey Audubon Society. Teams compete to identify the greatest number of bird species in a 24-hour period.
* [Migration Madness](/w/index.php?title=Migration_Madness&action=edit&redlink=1 "Migration Madness (page does not exist)"): A month-long festival celebrating bird migration. Migration Madness features a Birdathon. The Birdathon is a competition at any time [during](https://bubo.org/view-all-lists.html#listtop) May 2024. The goal is to spot as many bird species as you can.
* [Life List](https://bubo.org/view-all-lists.html#listtop)

Many birders keep a list of all the birds they have seen. Some lists are confined to particular geographical regions or time periods.

## Networking and organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Networking and organization")]

Prominent national and continental organizations concerned with birding include the [British Trust for Ornithology](/wiki/British_Trust_for_Ornithology "British Trust for Ornithology") and [Royal Society for the Protection of Birds](/wiki/Royal_Society_for_the_Protection_of_Birds "Royal Society for the Protection of Birds") in the United Kingdom, and the [American Birding Association](/wiki/American_Birding_Association "American Birding Association"), [Birds Canada](/wiki/Birds_Canada "Birds Canada") and the [Cornell Lab of Ornithology](/wiki/Cornell_Lab_of_Ornithology "Cornell Lab of Ornithology") in North America. Many statewide or local Audubon organizations are also active in the United States, as are many provincial and local organizations in Canada. [BirdLife International](/wiki/BirdLife_International "BirdLife International") is an important global alliance of bird conservation organizations. Many countries and smaller regions (states/provinces) have "rarities committees" to check, accept or reject reports of rare birds made by birders.

## Equipment and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Equipment and technology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Birdwatchers_Liminka_Bay_2006_04_14.JPG/250px-Birdwatchers_Liminka_Bay_2006_04_14.JPG)](/wiki/File:Birdwatchers_Liminka_Bay_2006_04_14.JPG)

Birders using a tower hide to gain views over foreground vegetation. Bay of Liminka, south of [Oulu](/wiki/Oulu "Oulu"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")

Equipment commonly used for birding includes [binoculars](/wiki/Binoculars "Binoculars"), a [spotting scope](/wiki/Spotting_scope "Spotting scope") with [tripod](/wiki/Tripod_(photography) "Tripod (photography)"), a [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), a notepad, and one or more [field guides](/wiki/Field_guide "Field guide"). Hides (known as *blinds* in North America) or observation towers are often used to conceal the observers from birds, and/or to improve viewing conditions. Virtually all optics manufacturers offer specific binoculars for birding, and some have even geared their whole brand to birders.

### Sound equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sound equipment")]

Recognition of [bird vocalizations](/wiki/Bird_vocalization "Bird vocalization") is an important part of a birder's toolkit. Sound information can assist in the locating, watching, identification, and sometimes sexing of birds. Recent developments in audio technology have seen [recording and reproduction](/wiki/Portable_audio "Portable audio") devices shrink in both size and price, making them accessible to a greater portion of the birding community.

The non-linear nature of digital audio technology has also made selecting and accessing the required recordings much more flexible than tape-based models. It is now possible to take a recording of every bird call you are likely to encounter in a given area out into the field stored on a device that will slip into your pocket and to retrieve calls for playback and comparison in any order you choose.

As the technology continues to improve, researchers and hobby birders have started using convolutional neural networks to mine sound recordings to identify and track specific bird calls.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Photography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Photography")]

[Photography](/wiki/Photography "Photography") has always been a part of birding, but in the past the cost of cameras with [super-telephoto lenses](/wiki/Super-telephoto_lenses "Super-telephoto lenses") made this a minority, often semi-professional, interest. The advent of affordable [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), which can be used in conjunction with a spotting scope or binoculars (using the technique of [afocal photography](/wiki/Afocal_photography "Afocal photography"), referred to by the [neologism](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") "*[digiscoping](/wiki/Digiscoping "Digiscoping")*" or sometimes *digibinning* for binoculars), have made this a much more widespread aspect of the hobby.

### Videography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Videography")]

As with the arrival of affordable [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), the development of more compact and affordable [digital video cameras](/wiki/Camcorder "Camcorder") has made them more attractive and accessible to the birding community. Cross-over, non-linear digital models now exist that take high-quality stills at acceptable resolutions, as well as being able to record and play audio and video. The ability to capture and reproduce not only the visual characteristics of a bird, but also its patterns of movement and its sound, has wide applications for birders in the field.

### Portable media players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Portable media players")]

This class of product includes devices that can play (and in some cases record) a range of digital media, typically video, audio and still image files. Many modern [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone"), and [camcorders](/wiki/Camcorder "Camcorder") can be classified as [portable media players](/wiki/Portable_media_player "Portable media player"). With the ability to store and play large quantities of information, pocket-sized devices allow a full birding multimedia library to be taken into the field and mobile Internet access makes obtaining and transmitting information possible in near real time.

### Remote birdwatching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Remote birdwatching")]

New technologies are allowing birdwatching activities to take place over the Internet, using robotic camera installations and mobile phones set up in remote wildlife areas. Projects such as CONE [[1]](https://web.archive.org/web/20080725090118/http://cone.berkeley.edu/) allow users to observe and photograph birds over the web; similarly, robotic cameras set up in largely inhospitable areas are being used to attempt the first photographs of the rare [ivory-billed woodpecker](/wiki/Ivory-billed_woodpecker "Ivory-billed woodpecker"). These systems represent new technologies in the birdwatcher's toolkit.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Communication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Communication")]

In the early 1950s, the only way of communicating new bird sightings was through the postal system and it was generally too late for the recipients to act on the information. In 1953 James Ferguson-Lees began broadcasting rare bird news on the radio in Eric Simms' *Countryside* program but this did not catch on. In the 1960s people began using the telephone and some people became hubs for communication. In the 1970s some cafés, such as that in [Cley, Norfolk](/wiki/Cley_next_the_Sea "Cley next the Sea") run by Nancy Gull, became centers for meeting and communication. This was replaced by telephone hotline services such as "Birdline" and "Bird Information Service".[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004267–275-49)

With the advent of the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web"), birders have been using the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") to convey information; this can be via [mailing lists](/wiki/Electronic_mailing_lists "Electronic mailing lists"), [forums](/wiki/Internet_forum "Internet forum"), [bulletin-boards](/wiki/Bulletin_board_systems "Bulletin board systems"), web-based [databases](/wiki/Database "Database") and other [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) While most birding lists are geographic in scope, there are special-interest lists that cater to bird-identification, 'twitchers', seabirds and raptor enthusiasts to name but a few. Messages can range from the serious to trivial, notifying others of rarities, questioning the taxonomy or identification of a species, discussing field guides and other resources, asking for advice and guidance, or organizing groups to help save habitats.

Occasional postings are mentioned in academic journals and therefore can be a valuable resource for professional and amateur birders alike.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) One of the oldest, *Birdchat*[[54]](#cite_note-54) (based in the US), probably has the most subscribers, followed by the English-language fork of *Eurobirdnet*,[[55]](#cite_note-55) Birding-Aus[[56]](#cite_note-56) from Australia, SABirdnet[[57]](#cite_note-57) from South Africa and Orientalbirding.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Mobile applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Mobile applications")]

The increasing availability of mobile devices in the 2010s allowed the smartphone to become a useful tool for birding. Mobile apps can be used as replacements for physical birding field guides, such as the digital version of the *[Sibley Guide to Birds](/wiki/The_Sibley_Guide_to_Birds "The Sibley Guide to Birds")* and the official Audubon Society app.[[59]](#cite_note-:1-59) Other apps utilize [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") to automatically identify birds from photographs and audio recordings, such as the Cornell Lab of Ornithology's [Merlin Bird ID](/wiki/Cornell_Lab_of_Ornithology#Merlin_Bird_ID "Cornell Lab of Ornithology") application and [iNaturalist](/wiki/INaturalist "INaturalist").[[59]](#cite_note-:1-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

[Cornell Lab of Ornithology](/wiki/Cornell_Lab_of_Ornithology "Cornell Lab of Ornithology")'s [eBird](/wiki/EBird "EBird") database is a popular tool used by birders to document their sightings. In addition to serving as a [citizen science](/wiki/Citizen_science "Citizen science") project used by ornithologists to document trends in bird populations,[[61]](#cite_note-61) it allows birders see recent reports by other birders and search by species and location.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Some species, including endangered species and others likely to be disrupted by increased human activity, are designated "sensitive species" by eBird and have locations of sightings hidden from the general public.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Code of conduct

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Code of conduct")]

As the numbers of birdwatchers increases, there is growing concern about the impact of birdwatching on the birds and their habitat. Birdwatching etiquette is evolving in response to this concern.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Some examples of birdwatching etiquette include promoting the welfare of birds and their environment, limiting use of photography, [pishing](/wiki/Pish "Pish") and playback devices to mitigate stress caused to birds, maintaining a distance away from nests and nesting colonies, and respecting private property.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The lack of definite evidence, except arguably in the form of photographs, makes birding records difficult to prove but birdwatchers strive to build trust in their identification.[[66]](#cite_note-66) One of the few major disputes was the case of the [Hastings Rarities](/wiki/Hastings_Rarities "Hastings Rarities").

## Socio-psychology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Socio-psychology")]

See also: [Biophilia hypothesis](/wiki/Biophilia_hypothesis "Biophilia hypothesis")

[Ethologist](/wiki/Ethology "Ethology") [Nikolaas Tinbergen](/wiki/Nikolaas_Tinbergen "Nikolaas Tinbergen") considers birdwatching to be an expression of the male hunting instinct, while [Simon Baron-Cohen](/wiki/Simon_Baron-Cohen "Simon Baron-Cohen") links it with a male tendency for "systemizing".[[67]](#cite_note-67) There have been suggestions that identification of birds may be a form of gaining status which has been compared with [Kula valuables](/wiki/Kula_ring "Kula ring") noted in Papua New Guinean cultures.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

A study of the motivations for birdwatching in New York concluded that initial motivations were largely similar in males and females, but males who participate actively in birding are more motivated by "sharing knowledge" with others, and active female birders are more motivated by their "intellectual" interest in studying birds, and by the "challenge" of identifying new and rare birds and improving their skills.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Another study suggested that males lean towards competitive birding, while females prefer recreational birdwatching.[[70]](#cite_note-70) A study for birdwatchers in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") found that the proportion of female birdwatchers involved in twitching has grown in recent years and that female birdwatchers were more willing to participate in observations of more common bird rarities than male birdwatchers.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) While the representation of women has always been low,[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004316–330-71) it has been pointed out that nearly 90% of all birdwatchers in the United States are white, with only a few African Americans.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Other minority groups have formed organizations to support fellow birders, such as the Gay Birders Club[[73]](#cite_note-73) and Birding For All, formerly the Disabled Birders Association.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004316–330-71)[[74]](#cite_note-74)

The study of birdwatching has been of interest to students of the sociology of science.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) A 2024 study conducted in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") examined how [personality traits](/wiki/Personality_traits "Personality traits") predict birdwatching interest and self-identification as a birdwatcher. The results highlighted that [openness to experience](/wiki/Openness_to_experience "Openness to experience"), [agreeableness](/wiki/Agreeableness "Agreeableness"), and [conscientiousness](/wiki/Conscientiousness "Conscientiousness") positively influenced engagement, while [neuroticism](/wiki/Neuroticism "Neuroticism") had a negative effect.[[75]](#cite_note-75) People who have nature-based experiences report better well-being and lower psychological distress than those who do not, and birdwatching in particular was found to have higher gains in subjective well-being and more reduction in distress than more generic nature exposure, such as walks.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Famous birdwatchers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Famous birdwatchers")]

See also: [List of birdwatchers](/wiki/List_of_birdwatchers "List of birdwatchers")

There are more than 11,000 species of bird and only a small number of people have seen more than 7,000. Many birdwatchers have spent their entire lives trying to see all the bird species of the world.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The first person who started this is said to be [Stuart Keith](/wiki/Stuart_Keith "Stuart Keith").[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004261-79)

Birders have been known to go to great lengths and some have lost their lives in the process. [Phoebe Snetsinger](/wiki/Phoebe_Snetsinger "Phoebe Snetsinger") spent her family inheritance travelling to various parts of the world while suffering from a [melanoma](/wiki/Melanoma "Melanoma"), surviving an attack and rape in New Guinea before dying in a road accident in [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar").[[80]](#cite_note-80) She saw as many as 8,400 species. The birdwatcher [David Hunt](/wiki/David_Hunt_(ornithologist) "David Hunt (ornithologist)") who was leading a bird tour in [Corbett National Park](/wiki/Corbett_National_Park "Corbett National Park") was killed by a tiger in February 1985.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) In 1971, [Ted Parker](/wiki/Theodore_A._Parker_III "Theodore A. Parker III") (who later died in an air crash in Ecuador) travelled around North America and saw 626 species.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004242-83) This record was beaten by [Kenn Kaufman](/wiki/Kenn_Kaufman "Kenn Kaufman") in 1973 who travelled 69,000 miles and saw 671 species and spent less than a thousand dollars.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss2004240–241-84)

In 2012, [Tom Gullick](/wiki/Tom_Gullick "Tom Gullick"), an Englishman who lives in Spain, became the first birdwatcher to log over 9,000 species.[[85]](#cite_note-85) In 2008, two British birders, Alan Davies and Ruth Miller, gave up their jobs, sold their home and put everything they owned into a year-long global birdwatching adventure about which they a wrote a book called *The Biggest Twitch*. They logged their 4,341st species on 31 December 2008, in Ecuador.[[86]](#cite_note-86) [Noah Strycker](/wiki/Noah_Strycker "Noah Strycker") recorded 6,042 species during 2015, overtaking Davies and Miller.[[87]](#cite_note-87) In 2016, Arjan Dwarshuis became the world-record holder for most species seen during the span of one year, logging 6,852 bird species in 40 countries.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

In early February 2024, [Peter Kaestner](/wiki/Peter_Kaestner "Peter Kaestner") became the first birder to log over 10,000 species, a record surrounded by much controversy as he was initially claimed to be beaten by Jason Mann who later conceded defeat.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Birdwatching literature, field guides, and television programs have been popularized by birders such as [Pete Dunne](/wiki/Pete_Dunne_(author) "Pete Dunne (author)") and [Bill Oddie](/wiki/Bill_Oddie "Bill Oddie").

## In media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birdwatching&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: In media")]

The 2011 film *[The Big Year](/wiki/The_Big_Year "The Big Year")* depicted three birders competing in an [American Birding Association](/wiki/American_Birding_Association "American Birding Association") Area [big year](/wiki/Big_year "Big year"), and the 2019 film *[Birders](/wiki/Birders_(film) "Birders (film)")* is a short documentary.
